coredata.dc:title: Selective storage and evolution of hydrogen on nafion/NaCl/graphene quantum dot mixed matrix using tensammetry as power electrochemical technique
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Deylaminezhad, Morteza
Zakipour, Mohammad
Doroodmand, Mohammad Mahdi
Mehrtash, Maryam
coredata.dc:description:
Hydrogen storage/evolution behavior of nafion/NaCl/graphene quantum dot (GQD) mixed matrix as selective hydrogen capacitor (power source) was evaluated in detail through an electrochemical process at two independent potential ranges. For this purpose, a three-electrode system included Pt disk as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and GQD-based mixed matrix-modified Pt disk as working electrode. For hydrogen storage, the deposition potential and time were evaluated to −1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and 120 s, respectively under high basic solution generated using NaOH (1.0 M) solution, followed by evolution of hydrogen at +0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) during formation of hydrogen bubbles. The main advantage of this system was the occurrence of hydrogen storage and evolution at two independent potential windows. Both mass transfer and adsorption processes were estimated for the tensammetric peak during the evolution step. The mechanism of hydrogen storage and evolution was obeyed from diffusion and tensammetry, respectively. According to Randles–Sevcik equation using 1.0 mM
Fe
( CN )
6
3 −
/
4 −
, the active surface area of nafion/NaCl/GQD mixed matrix was ∼1906 m2g−1. Based on the CHN analyses, pressure-concentration temperature as well as hydrogen temperature-programmed desorption, the capacity of the synthesized GQDs for hydrogen storage and evolution was estimated to at least 10.1 and 8.6 wt%, respectively. The stability of the electrode was also estimated during 7000s by chronoamperometry during applying at least 40 cycles in the range from −1.0 to +1.3 V with reproducible tensammetric peak current (relative standard deviation: 2.54%).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogen storage and evolution
Graphene quantum dot
Mixed matrix
Hydrogen capacitor
Electrochemical process
Tensammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of pH on the current
Active surface area of GQD-modified Pt electrode
Repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the electrode system
Effect of nafion on the stability of the electrode system
CHN analysis: storage/evolution capacity of GQDs

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the designed electrochemical cell.
Hydrogen storage and evolution of 50.0 mg of GQDs, CDs and their hybrid in water environment at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 in potential domain between −1.0 and +1.7 V.
Catalytic effects of 0.5 M chloride salts of cations such as Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+ and Cu2+ for hydrogen storage on 50.0 mg GQD at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 and accumulation time of 5.0 min.
CVs comparing peak currents, a) pure GQD (activated at potential of +1.0 V for 1.5 h); b), mixture of NaCl:GQD (activated at potential of +1.0 V for 10 s).
Effect of the pH of the electrolyte during H2 storage and evolution on GQD-modified Pt electrode at scan rate of 100 mV s−1, accumulation voltage of −1.0 V and accumulation time of 2.0 min.
Effect of different concentrations of OH− solution as electrolyte at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 and accumulation time of 2.0 min.
Effect of accumulation time ranging between 20–120 s during applying −1.0 V potential using 1.0 M OH− as electrolyte at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1.
Effect of different accumulation potentials using OH− solution (1.0 M) as electrolyte at scan rate of 100 mV s−1 and accumulation time of 2 min.
CVs of 1.0 M OH− solution on GQD-modified Pt with different scan rates in the range of 20–220 mV s−1.
Nyquist plot of GQD-modified Pt electrode in 1.0 M OH− at different electrode potentials, insets) the electronic circuit and Nyquist plots at various potentials.
Nyquist plots for bare Pt and GQD-modified Pt electrodes. Insets show electronic circuit and Nyquist plots for different electrodes at various potentials.
Parameters obtained from Nyquist plots of GQDs-modified Pt electrode in 1.0 M OH− at different electrode potentials.
Parameters obtained from Nyquist plots of bare Pt and GQDs-modified Pt electrodes in 1.0 M OH− at storage and evolution potentials.
The CHN analyses of GQDs in different conditions.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The bold data are the total capacity of the synthesized MMM for selective storage and evolution of hydrogen.

head.ce:title: Selective storage and evolution of hydrogen on nafion/NaCl/graphene quantum dot mixed matrix using tensammetry as power electrochemical technique

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shiraz University, Shiraz 71454, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mechanical Engineering Department, Sharif University of Technology, Azadi Ave, PO Box: 11365-11155, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Hydrogen storage/evolution behavior of nafion/NaCl/graphene quantum dot (GQD) mixed matrix as selective hydrogen capacitor (power source) was evaluated in detail through an electrochemical process at two independent potential ranges. For this purpose, a three-electrode system included Pt disk as counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as reference electrode and GQD-based mixed matrix-modified Pt disk as working electrode. For hydrogen storage, the deposition potential and time were evaluated to −1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) and 120 s, respectively under high basic solution generated using NaOH (1.0 M) solution, followed by evolution of hydrogen at +0.8 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) during formation of hydrogen bubbles. The main advantage of this system was the occurrence of hydrogen storage and evolution at two independent potential windows. Both mass transfer and adsorption processes were estimated for the tensammetric peak during the evolution step. The mechanism of hydrogen storage and evolution was obeyed from diffusion and tensammetry, respectively. According to Randles–Sevcik equation using 1.0 mM
Fe
( CN )
6
3 −
/
4 −
, the active surface area of nafion/NaCl/GQD mixed matrix was ∼1906 m2g−1. Based on the CHN analyses, pressure-concentration temperature as well as hydrogen temperature-programmed desorption, the capacity of the synthesized GQDs for hydrogen storage and evolution was estimated to at least 10.1 and 8.6 wt%, respectively. The stability of the electrode was also estimated during 7000s by chronoamperometry during applying at least 40 cycles in the range from −1.0 to +1.3 V with reproducible tensammetric peak current (relative standard deviation: 2.54%).

ce:section.ce:para: The global energy crisis has been rapidly evolved over the last few decades mainly due to the exhaustion of traditional energy and atmospheric pollution [1]. But the current principal challenge is to find new sources of energy which are cheaper and cleaner than fossil fuels [2]. Hydrogen (H2) is considered as an ideal energy and an alternative to fossil fuels because of its numerous advantages such as high energy density, high-quality clean, environmentally friendly, produced from an abundant renewable energy sources and promising as energy carrier for the future [3–5]. Compared to fossil fuel, the combustion of H2 only produces water, and offers their environmental benefits. Whereas the combustion of fossil fuel emits CO2 during its use [6–9].

There are several methods for H2 production; via electrolysis of water, splitting of water by light (photolysis), reforming gas from biomass, and natural gas or any other fossil fuel [10,11]. Electrochemical water splitting using renewable energy sources such as solar energy has attracted the attention of researchers [10,11]. Water electrolysis so far has been selected as the simplest method, developed to produce high quantities of H2 [10–14]. Nevertheless this method is considered as too expensive and consumes plenty of energies with directly proportional to the cell voltage used to perform the electrolysis. Therefore considerable researches have been conducted to enhance the electrocatalytic activity of electrodes to lower the overpotentials for the H2 generation [12–14]. For H2 storage, the main reaction in the water electrolysis is “Hydrogen Evolution Reaction” (HER) [15]. For the H2 storage the safety and efficiency of the H2 storage is a key requirement, however this process has remained the most challenging issues [16].

Based on the literature, compressing H2 at room temperature and high pressures up to between 350 and 700 bar, or its liquefaction at cryogenic temperatures and low pressures are considered as the initial methods for H2 storage. However high costs and the safety risks are significant drawbacks of these conventional storage techniques that have hindered their commercial applications so far [16]. Adsorbing hydrogen on a solid adsorbent at moderate pressures and ambient temperatures is another potential method for hydrogen storage. The method is selected as safer and requires less expensive storage equipment compared to high-pressure compression and cryogenic systems [17].

Synthesis of adsorbent materials for adsorbing large amounts of H2 at such conditions is the commercialization of H2 storage systems based on solid adsorbents [18–23]. Among these adsorbents metal hydrides and carbon-based materials are the two major types of adsorbents that have been considered to date as the subject of many studies in the field of H2 storage over the past years [18–27]. Hydrogen adsorption on carbon nanomaterials is relatively new field that has only been available in the last few decades. Initial work began with carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [28], followed by research on hydrogen adsorption of several different fullerenes and activated carbons (ACs) [29]. Based on the literature different capacities were estimated for these adsorbents depending on their synthetic methods [3–7]. For instance Ye et al. [9] found a gravimetric H2 storage capacity as high as 8.25 wt% using high purity single-walled CNTs (SWCNTs) at 80 K under a pressure of ca. 7 MPa. The highest capacity for H2 storage was reported to date by Chambers et al. at high critical conditions [12] using graphite nanofibres at ambient temperatures and at 11.35 MPa pressure. Electrochemical hydrogen storage was also carried out by Nützenadelet al. [13] on the surface of as-prepared SWCNTs and multi-walled CNTs. In the SWCNT support, the measured discharge capacity indicated storage capacity as large as 0.39 wt% (110 mA h g−1). In addition SWCNTs were considered as reversible charging capacities up to 800 mA h g−1 with capacity of 2.9 wt% [14]. Various studies on hydrogen storage have been reported in gas–solid medium and electrochemical system on the surface of graphite nanofibers (GNFs) and carbon nanofibers (CNFs) [2–6]. But in spite of high aspect ratio of these nanostructures, the capacity of H2 storage is still low [2–6].

Recent investigations show that the microstructures of carbon nanomaterials have an important influence on the hydrogen storage [10–13]. During the last decade, graphene due to its special properties has been selected as a huge H2 storage density and exploited to store H2 fuel technologies [30]. Graphene which was firstly discovered by A.K. Geim et al., in 2004 [31], is a monolayer with ∼0.34 nm thickness and sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [31–36]. In fact graphene forms the basic structure of other carbon-based materials such as graphite (stacked graphene), CNTs, graphene sheets and fullerene (wrapped up graphene) [36–40].

Among the graphene compounds, GQDs as a new kind of quantum dots have emerged and ignited tremendous research interest [41]. To control the bandgap of graphenes, a promising approach is to convert two dimensional (2-D) graphene sheets into zero dimensional (0-D) GQDs [42]. Consequently apart from unique electron transportation properties of GQDs [43], this kind of carbon allotrope has been selected as a new type of carbon dots (CDs) with unique properties associated with both graphene and CDs. Therefore many interesting phenomena are expected in view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects of 0-D GQDs [44,45]. The extraordinary properties of nanomaterials such as CDs and GQDs can be adopted in different fields of sciences such as solar cells, fuel cells, hydrogen storage/evolution, etc. [45]. Zero-dimensional nanostructures are also considered as promising materials adopted for energy storage applications [46–48]. For instance, the capacity of these carbon-based nanostructures for H2 storage can be promoted via doping atoms such as N or B or via formation of nanohybrids during modification with metal nanoparticles such as Ni, Mo, Cr, etc. [46–48]. However selective storage/desertion of H2 is considered as serious challenge for the economical purposes. For this purpose hereby in this study a new solid-based support has been introduced based on the storage and evolution of H2 via an applicable technology adopted as “Tensammetry”. Briefly tensammetry is an electrochemical technique in which the quantity of the capacitance current is evaluated during change in the morphology of the double layer of the working electrode.

Nanocarbon such GQDs, CDs and their hybrid (mixture) of CDs/GQDs were synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [49] through a facile sonoelectrochemical exfoliation method. For this purpose, an amperometric detection system has been selected during applying an optimum direct current (DC) bias (voltage: +8.50 V and current: 0.20 A) between a two-electrode system including a stainless steel electrode as cathode and a graphite rod as anode under optimized conditions such as 0.5 M NaCl at pH ∼7 [49]. Materials such as KCl, NaCl, KNO3, AgCl, AgNO3, CuCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, NaOH, and KOH were purchased from Merck company. All the reagents were from their analytical grades. 10.0 mL solution of 1.11 mM Fe(CN6)3− (Fluka) was prepared via dissolving 3.55 g K3[Fe(CN)6] inside a 10.0-mL volumetric flask and diluted to the mark using triply-distilled water. Also 10.0 mL solution of 1.00 M KCl (Merck) as supporting electrolyte was prepared via dissolving 0.75 g KCl using the triply-distilled water. To control the mechanical stability of the working electrode, NafionR with low molecular weight (0.5% solution in methanol) was used and purchased from “Sigma Company”.

A novel electrochemical cell was designed using glass cylindrical tubing with diameter of 5.0 cm (height) × 2.5 cm (radius). A conventional three-electrode system was used, consisting of a bare or modified Pt disk electrode (2.0 mm diameter) as working electrode, platinum disk as counter electrode and a saturated Ag/AgCl, Cl− (3.0 M, Metrohm) as a reference electrode. The counter and reference electrodes were introduced to the cell through a teflon tubing. Whereas the working electrode was positioned from the bottom of the glass cell. The inter-electrode distance of the electrodes was controlled via positioning the working electrode inside a glass flange. The cell assembly was schematically shown in Fig. 1 . All the electrochemical modes including cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and choronoamprometry were performed with a Potentiostat–Galvanostat, μAutolab type Ш. All experiments were conducted at 25 ± 2 °C. FT-IR spectra were recorded using Shimadzu (FTIR-8300) spectrophotometer.

The phase composition between the synthesized carbon nanostructures was also characterized by several techniques such as X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu XD-3A diffractometer, Cu Kα radiation, 0.15418 nm, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Zeiss-EM10C-80 KV, 200 kV), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, KYKY-EM3200), and XPS (ESCALAB 200-D). Analytical techniques such as pressure-concentration temperature as well as hydrogen temperature-programmed desorption were evaluated using a thermo-gravimetric analysis system (Metrohm).

To study the hydrogen storage and evolution behavior of GQD-based mixed matrix, the electrode was prepared by hand-mixing the GQDs powder and NaCl with 30:70 (GQD:NaCl, W/W%). Then, 0.1 g of this powder was directly inserted on the surface of the Pt disk as working electrode. To generate the mixed matrix, ∼1.0 μL solution of 0.5% nafionR in methanol as solvent was added. The GQD-modified Pt disk as working electrode was then conditioned via activation by applying a 1.0-V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl) for ∼10 s in KOH solution at pH ∼11.0.

After modification of the working electrode with GQD-based mixed matrix, the electrochemical studies were considered in 10.0 mL OH− (1.0 M) as electrolyte. This process was achieved using different electrochemical modes such as CV, EIS, amperometry, and chronoamperometry.

Hydrogen storage and evolution (HSE) has been considered as one of the most important topics since the last decade. This was due to the important role of H2 as safe, clean, low cost and efficient fuel, compared to the fuels such as nuclear or petroleum. This process was often occurred through the physico/chemical interactions between H2 molecules and absorbers. During the last decades, supports such as zeolite, AC, metal hydride, metal-organic frameworks, etc. have been introduced as general supports for H2 storage purposes. Recently, the importance of the nanostructure have been encouraged the scientists to focus on nanostructures such as nanocarbons, quantum dots, etc. Among these nanomaterials, carbon nanostructures such as CNTs were often used. This was due to high aspects ratio and presence of basal/edge planes in their matrix that made them appropriate for H2 storage. In spite of the fantastic characteristics of CNTs, problems related to the absorbers with ideal properties are still existed. To solve these problems, this study focuses on introducing some new nanostructures for H2 storage and evolution in an alkaline environment using Tensammetry.

These nanocarbons were synthesized according to selective method at large scale via sono-electrochemical exfoliation of graphite electrode thorough the intercalation of sodium chloride [49]. Fig. 1.SP shows the TEM and SEM images of each GQDs and CDs. As clearly shown, significant difference was observed between the morphologies of the SEM and TEM images. High resolution SEM (HRSEM) image of the mixed matrix has been shown in Fig. 1.SP. It should be noted that, mixed matrix due to possessing macroscopic shaping consist of mixture of organic, inorganic, and filler matrices, which provide a matrix with nano-sized dimension. Therefore the use of HRSEM does not show the particles with higher resolution. In addition, characterization of GQDs and CDs with spectroscopic techniques such as X-ray pattern (XRD, Fig. 2A.SP), Raman spectrometry (Fig. 2B.SP), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Fig. 2C, D.SP) clearly pointed to the difference in the nanostructure and morphology of GQDs and CDs, which were in good agreement with the results reported in Ref. [49]. For storage and evolution of H2 on the surface of these nanocarbons, parameters such as kind of nanostructures, optimum quantity of nanocarbon, kind and morphology of electrolyte, concentration of electrolyte, deposition time and accumulation voltage were optimized. Analytical factors such as active surface of area, repeatability, reproducibility, stability and capacity of nanostructures were evaluated and analyzed. Likewise some studies were done using semi-integrated voltammetry.

For HSE different forms of carbon nanostructures such as GQDs, CDs and their hybrid were used for modification of the working electrode. The CVs of different forms of nanocarbons such as GQDs, CDs, and their hybrids were shown in Fig. 2 . As clearly shown, H2 was electrochemically generated and stored on GQDs, CDs and their hybrid at the catholic potential wall of OH− at −1.0 V potential (vs. Ag/AgCl). The stored H2 was then evoluted at potential of max +1.1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). Comparison between the tensammetric currents in the CVs of each GQDs, CDs, and the hybrid (Fig. 2) revealed that the capacity of GQDs (ip = 212.0 μA) for HSE was significantly higher than that of hybrid (ip = 94.2 μA) and CD (ip = 62.3 μA). This was probably due to the presence of significant edge planes in the GQDs, compared to that of hybrid and CDs. Therefore GQD was selected as absorber via modification on the surface of the Pt working electrode.

The catalytic behavior of some inorganic species such as Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Na+ were investigated in detail. Choice of these species was based on the intercalating role of these reagents during the synthesis of the nanocarbons by the sonoexfoliation method [49]. For this purpose, ∼0.5 M of each inorganic species was added to the supporting electrolyte during using 50.0 mg GQD mixed matrix modified on Pt disk as the working electrode. The catalytic effect of these species was evaluated according to the CVs shown in Fig. 3 .

As clearly exhibited (Fig. 3), no catalytic effect was observed upon using the individual solution of Ag+, Co2+, Ni2+and Cu2+. About the Ag+, observation of and oxidative peak was probably related to the oxidation of Ag species that was reduced on the surface of the working electrode at the negative potential. However due to the lack of observation of any positive effect on the H2 generation, storage and evolution, it was not necessary to evaluate this peak in detail. The peak observed at the +1.2 V potential was tensammetric peak during the release of H2 bubble from the surface of the GQD-modified Pt electrode. The peaks observed at 0.35 and 0.06 V potentials were also related to the oxidation and reduction of silver, respectively. Since NaCl powder acted as intercalating agent during the synthesis of GQDs, CDs, and their hybrid, the catalytic behavior of these species were evaluated in detail. To reach this aim, different weight ratios of GQDs and NaCl were homogeneously mixed and adopted as modified-Pt working electrode. The CVs of different weight percentage ratios of NaCl:GQDs such as 0:100, 30:70, 50:50, and 70:30 (W/W%) were shown in Fig. 3.SP. Based on the results (Fig. 3.SP), by adding NaCl to the GQDs, significant shift was observed in the tensammetric potential from ∼+1.1 V to less positive potential) ∼+0.8 V, vs. Ag/AgCl). This catalytic phenomenon was attributed to the decrease in overpotential of the HER during the intercalation process and promotion in the geometric surface area of GQDs. As clearly shown (Fig. 3.SP), partially the same tensammetric peak currents were observed for all the tested NaCl:GQDs ratios. The more quantity of NaCl powder, the higher shift was observed in the tensammetric peak potential. Fouling in the electrode structure was occurred, when higher ratios of NaCl:GQDs such as 80:20 and 90:10 (W/W%) were used. Fouling the electrode was due to the formation of H2 bubbles on the surface of the electrode, resulting to have bare electrode. Therefore maximum potential shift (∼0.3 V potential) was experimentally observed for NaCl:GQDs with the weight percentage ratio of 70:30 (W/W%). Consequently this ratio was selected as the optimal value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the current
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value was another important factor having important effect on the performance of GQDs during studying the H2 storage process. For this purpose various pH values were provided using 0.2 M phosphate buffer solutions according to the voltammograms shown in Fig. 4 . As clearly observed in Fig. 5 , by increasing the pH of electrolyte solution, a negligible increase was observed in the H2 storage and evolution process. To store more amounts of H2 during creation of higher tensammetric current an alkaline solution (∼1.0 M) was prepared using KOH (Merck). At this condition surprisingly a noticeable change was observed in the tensammetric current from ∼400 μA during using buffer environment to ∼4 mA when using strong alkaline solution as electrolyte (Fig. 5). Based on the observations, under these conditions, the capacity of GQDs was so high that large amount of H2 bubbles were observed during H2 evolution at potential range between 0.5 and 1.1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl).

The effect of the concentration of OH− solution as electrolyte was also studied in detail. Regarding to CVs shown in Fig. 6 , the highest sensitivity (maximum tensammetric peak current) was obtained using an alkaline solution with ∼1.0 M concentration. By increasing the OH− concentration, the electrode stability fluctuated that caused fouling the working electrode system during formation of a large amount of H2 bubbles. Consequently solution of ∼1.0 M OH− was selected as the maximum practical concentration.

For H2 storage, −1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) potential was initially applied to the electrode with different accumulation time durations and then cyclic voltammetry experiments were performed. As shown in Fig. 7 by increasing the accumulation time, the quantity of the stored H2 in the GQD matrix was increased. However by enhancement in the time duration, the quantity of H2 stored became high but electrode fouling was occurred at ∼+1.1 V (vs. Ag/AgCl). For this reason, the proper time duration (deposition time) was estimated to be ∼2.0 min.

For electrochemical storage of H2 in the surface of GQDs, different potentials were applied to the electrode system. The results were shown in Fig. 8 . As clearly shown, maximum H2 evolution (the highest tensammetric peak current) was evaluated during applying accumulation potential the same as −1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The reduction process of OH− was occurred at a potential of −1.0 V which then caused the production of H2 on the surface of the GQDs. This H2 was then desorbed at +0.8 V via generation of bubbles.

To evaluate the performance of working electrode, the electrical behavior of H2 storage and evolution was studied via modification of the same quantity of GQDs (0.100 ± 0.001 g) on the surface of electrodes such as Pt and glassy carbon (GC). The voltammograms were shown in Fig. 4.SP. Based on the results hydrogen storage on the GQD-modified GC electrode increased the overpotential. Fig. 4.SP also showed the CVs of the H2 storage and evolution during modification of the working electrode. As was clearly observed, GQDs due to the possessing plenty of basal/edge planes as well as multi-layers in their matrix acted as suitable active sites that considered as excellent nanomaterials for H2 storage and evolution. As can be seen, the tensammetric peak current in the GQD-modified Pt electrode at potential of +0.8 V was estimated to be 3.4 mA, whereas this value was estimated to be 2.3 mA at potential of +1.23 V for GQD-modified GC electrode.

Cyclic voltammograms of 1.0 M KOH on the GQD-modified Pt electrode with different scan rates (20–220 mV s−1) were shown in Fig. 9 . During increasing the scan rate (ν), the peak current increased gradually. As is shown in Fig. 5.SP(a), the anodic peak current increased linearly with scan rate (mV s−1) in the range of 20–200 mV s−1 with a mathematical equation of y = 0.0144x + 0.0006, (R2 = 0.998). Also linear correlation was existed between the anodic peak current and the square root of scan rate with the correlation equation of y = 0.0091x - 0.0007, R2 = 0.9915 (Fig. 5.SP(b)). Also the log i
p vs. log ν graph was plotted (Fig. 5.SP(c)). The linear coefficient in this case was between 0.5 and 1.0. The behavior of the tensammetriy was probably attributed to high surface area of GQDs that significantly lowered the effect of scan rate during evaluation of the linear relationship between current and vs. scan rate or square root of scan rate. Additionally with increasing the scan rate, the peak potential (E
p ) shifted to positive potentials (from 0.74 V to 0.93 V), indicating that H2 evolution was partially an irreversible process due to the kinetic polarization.

Assuming that hydrogen was released as a result of a faradaic process, it was possible to calculate the parameters such as electron transfer coefficient (α) and surface coverage
( Г
). From the plot of E
p vs. log v, a slope (b) of 214.2 mV decade−1 was measured. Assuming one electron transfer in the rate determining step (n = 1), α value was calculated as 0.724 according to the Laviron's equations, which was valid for a totally irreversible diffusion-controlled process [50]. The relationship between the peak potential (E
p ) and the logarithm of scan rate (log v) for the GQD-modified Pt electrode in 1.0 M OH− was shown in Fig. 6.SP. The peak potential was changed linearly vs. log v with a linear regression equation of (E
p ) = 0.1071 log v + 0.9192 with R
2 = 0.996 in the range between 20 and 220 mV s−1. This indicated that the H2 evolution was an irreversible process.

In another experiment by plotting E
p versus log i, (Fig. 7.SP), the Tafel slope was obtained to be 128.0 mV decade−1 for the H2 storage/evolution reaction via using the data of the rising part of the current–voltage curve at 20 mV s−1scan rate. The α value was calculated as 0.461, which did not agree well with the value of transfer coefficient obtained in the previous paragraph. This result clearly showed the nonfaradaic behavior (tensammetry) of the peak current. Moreover, α value can be calculated from the difference between peak potential (E
P ) and half wave potential. At this condition the α value was calculated and found to be 0.726, which attributed well with the value of α obtained previously, i.e 0.724. This value again pointed to the irreversibility of the H2 storage process.

The concentration of electroactive species (
Г
) can be estimated by plotting the peak currents against the scan rate (20–220 mV s−1). The slope of this plot was 0.0144 (Fig. 5.SP(a)). From the slope of peak currents vs. scan rate, the calculated surface concentration of GQD-modified Pt electrode was found to be 1.22 × 10−7 mol cm−2. Low value of
Г
revealed less importance of the electron exchange process during the H2 evolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active surface area of GQD-modified Pt electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrochemical behaviors of GQD-modified Pt were investigated in a solution containing 1.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3− (Flucka) in 0.1 M KCl using cyclic voltammetry. The results were shown in Fig. 8.SP. A quasi-reversible redox behavior of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was observed for the GQD-modified Pt electrode. This process indicated i) suitable morphology of the electrode surface, ii) good conductivity of GQDs and iii) high surface areas of the electrode due to the modification of the electrode with GQDs. The peak current (i
p ) at GQD-modified Pt electrode (i
pa = 76 μA) was significantly higher than the bare Pt (i
pa = 3.25 μA), suggesting high sensitivity for GQD-modified Pt due to enhancement in the electroactive area of GQDs. By exploring the redox peak current and the scan rate (Fig. 9.SP), the average electroactive area of GQD-modified Pt electrode could be calculated using the Randles–Sevcik equation [51]. By applying the equation, the average electroactive surface area of GQD-modified Pt was found to be ∼1906 m2g−1. This result again revealed high active surface area of the synthesized GQDs. The mechanism of the hydrogen releasing was likely nonfaradaic because of incompatibility of α and
Г
values with the results obtained from the active surface area during measurement and the observation of the hydrogen bubbles at potential of +0.8 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the electrode system
ce:section.ce:para: Reproducibility was assessed by analyzing a solution containing 1.0 M OH− with GQD-modified Pt electrode. For nine replicate analyses under similar conditions, satisfactory relative standard deviation (RSD%) the same as 2.54% was obtained for the peak current of H2 storage/evolution process (Fig. 10.SP(a, b)). The repeatability of GQD-modified Pt was attained by testing the GQD-modified Pt for forty times with the RSD% of 2.14% (Fig. 10.SP(c)). These values indicated good reproducibility of the electrode for the H2 storage/evolution process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of nafion on the stability of the electrode system
ce:section.ce:para: To control the mechanical stability, the GQD-modified Pt electrode can also be modified with nafion. For this purpose, ∼1.0 μL of nafion was added and mixed with GQDs. After modifying GQDs with nafion, significant mechanical stability (at least for 100 potential scans) was observed from the electrode system without any electrode fouling during H2 storage and evolution. Stability of the electrode system was attributed to the formation mixed matrix. Briefly mixed matrix consists of three different phases including QGDs as inorganic phase, nafion as organic phase and NaCl as filler. The mixed matrix due to the possessing both organic and inorganic phases provided different characteristics such as high flexibility, acceptable permeability and excitedly selectivity [52,53]. In this system, NaCl played role as i) catalytic reagent during the tensammetric desorption of H2 bubbles from the surface of the GQD-modified working electrode and ii) filler (intercalating agent) during controlling the gap between the GQD layers. In another word, NaCl as filler inside the mixed matrix simply controlled the QGD layers (gaps) for selective storage of H2 inside the solid support. Although NaCl acted as intercalating agent, but formation of mixed matrix during interaction with nafion prevented the desorption and solubility of NaCl into the aqueous electrolyte. This effect significantly revealed the stability of the electrode system during the H2 storage and evolution.

According to the Nernst equation generation of H2 during the reduction process needs less negative potential. This behavior caused most of the previously reported articles in the literature review to focus on the H2 generation at the basic medium. However about some electrodes that are able to lower the over potential of the reduction of H+ to H2, acidic conditions have been selected, which have not been developed so much. The same behavior is also existed about the modified GQDs- Pt electrode in this work. Therefore lots of H2 bubbles are evaluated that may change the physical structure of the modified electrode. Consequently there was a direct relationship between the electrode stability and OH− concentration. However to solve this problem, the electrode system has been modified with nafion during formation of mixed matrix. Therefore the use of nafion was effective during providing modified support with higher mechanical stability.

The chronoamperometry of GQD-modified Pt electrode was shown in Fig. 11.SP. Two main regions were revealed from the amprogram: (i) the common gradual decrease in the current, which can be interpreted by the initial charging of the double layer on surface of the electrode (nonfaradaic current). Moreover due to the change in the electrode surface under hyrostatic condition, gradual depletion of diffusion layer would occur and (ii) the steady state region from 100 to 7000 s, which was attributed to the steady surface of electrode during diffusion of species towards the electrode surface. The steady state of the diffusion process could be attributed to the spherical diffusion at the electrode surface as well as to large surface area of the electrode.

Fig. 10 showed the impedance behavior of GQD-modified Pt electrode in different potentials by Nyquist plot at the optimum conditions. These plots were then fitted to Randless RCRW circuit and parameters such as C
dl , R
ct and R
S as shown in Table 1 . These investigations exhibited that maximum C
dl would be observed in −1.0 V potential at which maximum hydrogen storage was happened. Additionally, as it was shown in Table 1, at potentials between +0.8 and + 0.9 V hydrogen evolution was happened and C
dl was decreased. This behavior was attributed to the change in capacitance of the electrode system during the H2 storage and evolution. Impedance behavior of GQD-modified Pt and bare Pt electrodes were shown in Fig. 11 at the optimum conditions. Parameters such as C
dl , R
ct and R
S have been calculated by fitting corresponding Nyquist plots to Randless RCRW circuit (Table 2 ). As it was shown in Table 2, C
dl for modified electrode was larger than that of bare Pt electrode. According to the EIS, there was reverse relationship between the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the Cdl. Therefore the higher quantity of the Cdl, the lower was the Rct. Thisindicated that, at higher degree of Cdl, the lower Rct caused to have simpler charge transfer process is provided. Therefore the GQD-modified pt electrode should have higher Cdl compared to that of the bare Pt electrode.
This showed higher capacity of the modified electrode for hydrogen storage application in various fields such as fuel cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CHN analysis: storage/evolution capacity of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As hydrogen storage and evolution in the GQD support was selectively dependent to the applied potential of the electrode system, therefore it was possible to evaluate the hydrogen storage/evolution using analytical methods such as CHN analysis. The CHN was therefore a semi-analytical method for estimating the percentage of C, H, N and O atoms in the samples. However due the conditioning the samples via washing and heating prior the CHN analysis, this technique simply led to semi-analytically estimate the quantity of hydrogen in the GQD matrix. As shown in Fig. 12.SP, the modified electrode was analyzed at different steps: a) fresh GQDs, b) after hydrogen storage on GQD, and c) after hydrogen evolution on GQDs as shown in Table 3 . As the CHN analysis pointed to the imperial formula of the GQD, therefore it was simply possible to evaluate the percentages of carbon and hydrogen based on the chemical formula of GQD at which there was mostly hydrogen and carbon atoms in its matrix. This calculation was achieved via estimation of hydrogen and carbon percentages based on 100% percentage of both hydrogen and carbon atoms. The results were shown in Table 3. According to the CHN analyses, the capacity of the synthesized GQDs for hydrogen storage and evolution was estimated to at least 10.1 and 8.6 wt%, respectively. These results were also confirmed by pressure-concentration-temperature (PCT) and hydrogen temperature-programmed desorption (H2 TPD) measurements as shown according to the diagrams shown in Fig. 13A and B.S.P. It should be noted that due to obeying the hydrogen evolution from tensammetry, it was impossible to estimate hydrogen storage/evolution using electrochemical techniques such as chronoqoulonometry [54]. Based on these results, hydrogen storage and evolution reaction had significantly been performed. Large capacity of GQDs for hydrogen storage/evolution was further evidenced according to the video film in the supporting information.

The well-known reaction steps in alkaline solutions were based on Eq. (1) [55], where R was the ideal gas constant, T was the absolute temperature, α ≈ 0.5 was the transfer coefficient and F was the Faraday constant. For cathodic HER, this step was followed by either an electrochemical desorption step (Heyrovsky reaction) (Eq. (2)), and/or a recombination step (Tafel reaction) (Eq. (3)) [55].
(1)
H 2
O +
e −

H

OH
−
( Volmer
step )
(2)
b =

α F
∼ 120
mV

ads
+
H 2

→
H 2
+
HO −
( Heyrovsky
reaction )
(3)

2.3
R T
(
1 +
α
)
F
∼
40
mV
H
ads
+
H
ads
→
H 2
( Tafel
reaction )

The Tafel slope was an inherent property of a catalyst determined by the rate-limiting step (rate determining step or rds) of the HER [56]. The determination and interpretation of the Tafel slope were also important for elucidation of the elementary steps. It has been widely accepted that the value of the charge-transfer coefficient, α, was dependent to the rds for multi-step reactions [57,58]. According to the observation of H2 bubbles as well as CHN and EIS analytical data, storage/evolution of hydrogen were observed at potentials of −1.0 and +0.8 V, respectively. Suggested mechanism for the desorption process was shown in Eq. (4). The physical sense of this mechanism was further evaluated by the video film. Formation of H2 gas was evidenced by both gas chromatography and plasma-based ionization gas sensor [59]. Based on this mechanism the evolution of hydrogen obeyed from tensammetry. Based on this mechanism, the introduced GQDs played role as appropriate H2 capacitor and carrier.
(4)
b =
2.3
R T
2 F
∼ 30
mV
2 H
ads
→
H 2
( gas )

Based on the literature, the standard gaseous storage of H2 on the pure carbon materials was estimated to be around 1.5 wt% [61,62]. Whereas this value reached to ∼4 wt% during various physicochemical processes such as formation of N- or G-doped GQDs [61,62], combination of low-dimensional nanomaterials with hybrid nanostructure [61,62], generation of GQD-metal composites [62] such as composite between GQDs and metal species and CdS [62], ZnO [63], SnO2 [64], Li [65], Ca [65], Co [65], etc. [61,65]. The HER can also be promoted up to ∼5.5 wt% [61] at critical conditions such as high pressure and low temperature. Based on the calculations for having carbon nanostructure with acceptable H2 storage behavior as power source at room temperature, factors such as production of bulk graphene samples improved the graphene's flexibility and unique electronic properties that provided new approaches for H2 storage purposes [61]. The calculation also proposed that, the HER properties of carbon nanostructures might be promoted up to ∼8 wt% during formation of multi-layer graphenes with enough inter-layer separation (between 6 and 8 Å) [61]. According to the density functional theory, the HER would be estimated up to ∼8.3% depending on the HC binding on the local curvature, which majorly provided plenty of basal/edge planes as active cites that caused high active surface area of the carbon nanostructures during the physic/chemical process [61,65,66]. However these processes caused significant promotion majorly in the oxygen reduction and CO2 formation processes [60,61,64], but formation of energy power source with acceptable H2 storages behaviors has still been considered with challenges [67]. The novelty of this study was based on factors such as i) high active surface area of the GQDs (∼1906 m2 g−1), which was not comparable even with Pt support (Fig. 4.SP), ii) multi-layer morphology of GQDs with enough inter-layer distance, iii) catalytic behavior of NaCl as green catalyst and intercalating agent (catalyst), iv) mixed matrix property of nafion/NaCl/GQDs, and v) tensammetric method through the intercalation process, that provided excellent support for both selective storage and revolution of H2.

In this study, carbon nanomaterials such as GQDs, CDs and their hybrids as excellent and selective power sources have been used for storage and evolution of hydrogen. The results showed that the active surface area of GQDs was very high for hydrogen storage process during the tensammetric voltammetry. The active surface area has been also investigated with SIV. As a result compared to other carbon allotropes, GQD was supposed to have acceptable properties for fuel cell applications. Based on the CHN analyses, the capacity of the synthesized GQDs for hydrogen storage and evolution was estimated to at least 10.1 and 8.6 wt%, respectively. Acceptable H2 storage/evolution was evaluated for GQDs compared to the previous reports which often estimated these values less than ∼5%. For instance, H2 intake capacity of carbon-based nanomaterials cannot exceed 1.5 wt%. Besides, the department of energy target is only 5.5 wt% at room temperature and moderate pressures of hydrogen gas. But about this mixed matrix, NaCl as filler inside the mixed matrix simply controlled the QGD layers (gaps) for selective storage of H2 inside the solid support. This process led to have acceptable and selective H2 capacity/desorption process for the synthesized mixed matrix. To the best of our knowledge this study is the first research that reported the tensammetry behavior of H2(gas) during controlling the H2 storage/evolution at large scale. Final conclusion was that, the synthesized GQDs acts electrochemically as a safe and controllable capacitor for H2 storing, H2 carrying and H2 evolution at large scale.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to acknowledge the support of this work by Shiraz University Research Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2017.02.067
